title: Bahrain Sprint: Dubišar Dominates from Pole as Grunt's Championship Lead Crumbles
meta: Published: 20 March 2026 | By Samira Al-Rashid, Emerging Talent Scout | F1CC News
caption: Marek Dubišar celebrates a lights-to-flag victory in the Bahrain sprint.
body:
Marek Dubišar arrived in Bahrain carrying the weight of a difficult Melbourne weekend — a quiet 11th place, a retirement from the feature race, and a championship table that didn't yet know his name. He left the sprint race with a pole position, a lights‑to‑flag victory, and the look of a driver who has just ripped up the script. The Invicta Racing driver, a Czech with the calm eyes of a natural and the backstory of a boy who learned to race on frozen karting circuits in the Moravian winter, delivered the most complete weekend of any driver in the 2026 F2 field so far. Mark my words: Marek Dubišar is not a flash. He is the real thing, and Bahrain was the moment he stopped being a promising name and became a championship contender.

The sprint qualifying format — three knockout sessions, no room for error — produced drama from the first flying lap. In SQ1, Dubišar set the fastest time of 1:33.156, a benchmark that would hold through the session and send a ripple of surprise through the paddock. Korv Batt, the Singaporean Prema driver whose smooth style has made him a favourite of the Mercedes academy, followed with a 1:33.265. Colin Hongnoi (Rodin Motorsport), Hamish Porter (Invicta), and Corentin Premeur (ART Grand Prix) rounded out the top five. But the real story was the elimination zone. Jaromir Grunt, the Australian feature race winner and championship leader, could only manage a 1:34.286 — good for 19th. The Czech, whose Melbourne heroics had made him the early season talisman, encountered traffic into Turn 1 on his best lap and never recovered. He climbed out of the AIX Racing car and stared at the timing screen for a long moment, the expression of a driver who understood exactly how much damage a single bad qualifying session can do.

SQ2 tightened the field further. Marcos Olmedo (Campos) went fastest, followed by Batt and Takumi Aizawa. But Colin Hongnoi, who had been third in SQ1, struggled and was eliminated, along with Daniel Park (Trident) and Hamish Porter, whose Invicta teammate Dubišar continued to set the pace. Frederik Frid (Van Amersfoort Racing) jumped to second late in the session, but Dubišar again topped the timesheets — a statement of consistency that echoed Tim Mittens' Melbourne weekend, but with the added steel of a driver who knew pole was within reach.

SQ3 was eight minutes of pure, unfiltered tension. The nine remaining drivers had one shot each on soft tyres. Korv Batt spun early, a heartbreaking end to a session that had promised much; he would start ninth. Yann Asdelian (DAMS), the French Alpine protégé who had clawed his way through SQ1 and SQ2, laid down a 1:32.150 that looked competitive. Then came Dubišar. His lap was not a single burst of genius but a symphony of perfectly timed apexes. He crossed the line with a 1:31.923 — nearly two‑tenths clear of Asdelian — and the Invicta garage erupted. Frederik Frid completed the top three with a 1:32.227. The grid was set: Dubišar on pole, Asdelian alongside, Frid in third, with Ryan Melon (ART), Tim Mittens (Hitech), Takumi Aizawa, Marcos Olmedo, Kodiak Callahan, and the unfortunate Batt filling the top nine. A rookie on pole. A field of talent behind him. And a 19‑lap sprint race waiting under the desert sun.

The sprint race began with Dubišar holding the lead into Turn 1, his Invicta chassis planted on the medium compound. Asdelian and Frid closed initially, but the order began to settle until Frid made a decisive move on Asdelian for second place — a lunge around the outside of Turn 3 that required absolute commitment and a car that responded to every input. From there, the front two settled into a rhythm. Mid‑race, the field was shaken by a collision between Jacob Vavro (AIX Racing) and Corentin Premeur, the two tangling under braking at Turn 8. Vavro received a 5‑second penalty; Premeur limped back to the pits with damage that would drop him to 14th.

The chaos opened a strategic window. In a near‑synchronised sequence, the entire field pitted — unusual for a sprint race, where track position usually trumps fresh rubber. Dubišar and Frid emerged still 1‑2, their crews delivering quick stops, while behind them the order shuffled. Tim Mittens, the American whose consistency had been a hallmark of Melbourne, suffered a heavy lock‑up and dropped down the order, his tyres flat‑spotted. Jaromir Grunt, already deep in the pack, spun at Turn 12 and finished well outside the points, completing a nightmare weekend that saw the championship leader score nothing. A collision between VAR teammates Colin Hongnoi and Hamish Porter earned Porter a 5‑second penalty that would later drop him from 16th to 19th, a gut‑wrenching end for the driver who had finished second in Australia.

In the closing laps, the fight for the podium intensified. Ryan Melon, the McLaren junior at ART Grand Prix, made two daring overtakes — first on Mittens, then on Asdelian — to move into fifth, his racecraft on full display. Frederik Frid, the VCARB Academy driver, briefly drew alongside Dubišar for the lead into Turn 1, a moment that made the Invicta garage hold its collective breath. But Dubišar repassed him into Turn 3, a defensive masterclass that spoke of a driver who would not be intimidated. He crossed the line 3.546 seconds clear of Frid, with Kodiak Callahan (DAMS) a distant third after a quiet but effective drive.

The final classification, once post‑race penalties were applied, showed Dubišar on the top step, followed by Frid, Callahan, Olmedo, Melon, Asdelian, Mittens, Batt, Thompson, and Park rounding out the top ten. Alex Thompson, the Mercedes junior splitting his time between F2 and the GT3 Spring Challenge, scored his first points of the season with a composed ninth, a result that felt like a small but significant step. Daniel Park (Trident) took the final point in tenth, the Korean driver showing the consistency that had made him a practice pacesetter in Australia. Further back, Jaromir Grunt finished 17th, and the paddock whispered that the championship had just been blown wide open.

The deep implication of Bahrain is not simply that Dubišar won. It is that the F2 field now has a new front‑runner, and the narrative of the season — Grunt's dominance, Invicta's inconsistency — has been rewritten in a single afternoon. Dubišar's performance was built on the kind of precision that wins championships: a pole lap that extracted every hundredth from the car, a race that managed every phase, and a defence that never wavered. His journey to this moment is the kind that fuels this paddock. A boy from the Czech Republic, his father a mechanic who worked on rally cars, his mother a schoolteacher who saved for his first kart. He won the Italian F4 championship in 2024, then stepped up to F3 and fought through a difficult season with the kind of quiet resilience that makes you believe he can survive anything. Now, in the Invicta colours, he has a car that responds to his touch and a team that believes in him. Bahrain was the proof of concept. The feature race still awaits, but for one desert evening, Marek Dubišar was the king of the sprint. The championship leader's crown has slipped. The chase is on. And somewhere in the Invicta garage, as the sun set over the Sakhir circuit, a young Czech driver sat in his cockpit a moment longer than necessary, the engine still ticking, and allowed himself a small, private smile. The future had arrived, and it was wearing his name.
